author: @tonypalmentera7752
text:
Robert, thanks for another great video. I have a modest suggestion for a video. I have recently learned that a closed and sealed 700 acre landfill in California supplies 70,000 homes with electricity. This is about one acre for every 100 homes. This landfill has enough gas to run for 50 years, from the date of its closing in 2013. The landfill was open for just over 50 years. So, maybe you already covered this and I missed it, as you do so many great videos for us...but if not, what about the idea of a trash battery in the form of a landfill? Filling it for 50 years is charging it, and you discharge for 50 years. If you removed plastics and aluminum, etc. from the trash deposited there, the entire thing will have completely broken down in that 100 years, leaving it open for possible construction of some types, and even possibly some shallow rooted permaculture. I wonder how it scales...like would a 20 acre landfill created by about 25 households for 50 years, return a yield of 100 households per acre annually, or 2,000 households worth of electricity for 50 years (proportional in scale to the California landfill)? If so, if we view landfills as a battery, the efficiency of the battery is an insane 80 output to 1 input, or 8000%. 

I must have done the math wrong...right? I realize there are inefficiencies I'm ignoring, like the running of the place while open, the drainage pumps and disposal, the 30 years of maintenance after, the building and running of the gas power plant for 50 years, and the diminished returns on gas production past peak production years. However, with just 1.25% capacity it can still power the original 25 homes that filled/charged it (if my math was remotely correct, of course). 

Anyway, thanks for the inspiring videos. Have a great September.

EDIT: I realize an error in my math already...I looked up the uncompacted cubic feet of waste, not compacted, so 25 households would not fill a 20 acre landfill in 50 years, I'm guessing. However, I wonder how few homes it would take to fill that many acres, given 4.5-5 pounds of trash per American per day. It may not be 80 to 1, but that's a lot of room for error before it's not profitable or efficient, even with all the inefficiencies I acknowledged, and more.

replies.author: @tonypalmentera7752
replies.text:
I further considered the advantage of constant gas, versus inconsistent solar/wind, so this is either an alternative to them, or in competition with coal and natural gas as the supporting constant power source when sun/wind is not available. I considered also the lack of need for as many or any battery infrastructure, the pollution being a sunk cost in the landfill, and the pollution for solar panels, turbines, and battery production not being a sunk cost. I considered as well ways to mitigate issues, such as combination with pump storage, and also the idea of surrounding a LFG power plant with many smaller landfills, like 12 for clock positions, and not only sharing the cost of the power plant per landfill, but also operating for 390-650 years (by opening one landfill for 30-50 years, then when it closes, opening another, and so on). 

I considered permaculture as more added value to the bottom line numbers. I also thought of tire collection for free materials for mechanical concrete, which is superior to normal roadway construction, and with free tired, might actually be as cheap or cheaper than normal road construction. Lastly, I considered the gas byproducts, and how it's worse than coal...however, we already burn this gas to mitigate the issue, whether we use it for power or not (again, the charging material and energy for the battery, and the pollution, is a sunk cost)...we also can already capture byproduct methane, burn it for electricity as well, increasing efficiency. At face value, it seems it's worse than coal...but is it when we consider everything?

author: @denisaredwine7426
text: Well I have a question..in your video you said 2 deep on the magnets, but the flywheel (magnet holder) isn't deep enough for two magnets because the clearance between the magnet and the bracket causes the wheel to NOT turn..do I need to modify the flywheel magnet holder for two magnets deep (which would take 64 magnets instead of the 32 specified on thingyverse), or were you referring to two magnets side by side in the N-S configuration?  I am experimenting with printed 608 bearings..which should work since the berings only keep the axel aligned..but the flywheel magnet holder seems to ride on the flywheel hub..is that the way you intended?  The axel hardly turns when the flywheel is 'pushed'..should the tolerance between the axel and the flywheel hub be a friction fit..or glued?  Thanks for an interesting project as an introduction to pulse motors...Cheers

author: @kanwarbhatti8924
text:
Hello,
I tried making one but it only rocks back and forth very weakly  a few times and stops, magnets were placed  as NS- NS -NS, clock pendulum  movement is the same that  you used,  Any thing you suggest i am not doing right

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: check the distance of the coil from the magnets - make it as close as you can get - and check the bearings - make sure it rests only on the needle point the top two bearings as guides only - but make sure they are free running - you might want to replace them with ceramic bearings - for skates these are cheap

replies.author: @denisaredwine7426
replies.text: I made one also that doesn't repel all the way for some reason...the author must be busy, cause I haven't heard from him on my question...anyway, how did you arrange your magnets, i.e. size of each magnet (RMS specified a 1x5x10 magnet and I used a 3x5x10). Also in the video he said something that I'm not sure of..that he doubled the magnets, i.e. putting them two deep.  If this was the case then the number of magnets specified is not correct (he specified 32, if he doubled them it would be..obviously 64).  Also doubling the magnets two deep would make the magnet size 2x5x10, since I used a 3x5x10 my pulse motor should work, but it doesn't .  I have tried increasing the voltage (added battery in series 3v versus 1.5--didnt help).  Since there were two pendulums in the Amazon pack, I took the pulse board and tried to simulate two pulsars, but that didnt work either)..so that's where Im at for now until I can figure something else out..cheers

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Hiya mate - I don't often reply to comments more than a few days old - 2 actually - as i get caught up with other things - i get around 4-500 emails, posts and comments everyday - so it is hard to keep up - anyway, my apologies and I spotted this - so, it is sensitive to any out of true or excess friction as you would expect with such low power - it is also sensitive to position of pendulum pulser - one neat trick is to not fix the pulser but hole it in place with your finger until you find the sweet spot - try a very slight angle until you find it - then when found glue it down - hope this helps

author: @arielmarshall5501
text: Greatings all. I am an amateur designer wondering if anyone can kindly reccomend a efficient mechanism for mechanically combining/accumulating multiple sources of low torque motion into a single higher torque output? Ideally without heavy or bulky wind, water/air pressure components, or electronics. Basically doing the same task as combining the torque output of multiple  wind turbines spinning at differing variable speeds.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: i will have to think about that - cheers - but immediately a differential come to mind

replies.author: @arielmarshall5501
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Hi Robert. Thanks for responding and for mentioning the differential. One can combine the output gears from a number of sources to drive a single gear, but this configuration would have poor efficiency, as the driven gear will generally be driven by the most powerful driving gear, rather than accumulating the force of the combined inputs. Really I think one would require an intermediary energy storage mechanism and to use air/water/electricity to feed it. I cannot think of any ways to do this without using these elements and without using any bulky/ heavy mechanisms, which as I mentioned is not ideal for many applications including my own, but I have come up with a mechanism that would efficiently accumulate wind power energy from multiple turbines and redirect it to a single output. If you like I will send you the .stl files when I have finished modelling it and you can share it with the community.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: that would be awesome mate if you would - i would really appreciate it

replies.author: @arielmarshall5501
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkeringI do not use discord, so once it is modelled, I will have to message you again in the comment sections of one of your video uploads. If you think of a small scale and lightweight, intermediary mechanical energy accumulation mechanism, that does not use water or electricity, please do share also.

author: @omnipresent2056
text: This is also the core concept behind things like CERN, where you have timed propulsion putting centrifugal force into a fixed wheel shape. Also rail guns use this same timed propulsion where the magnetic phase change coordinates with next coil in line giving the projectile a linear or exponential velocity. In a reverse as a generator or charge dump these have the greatest potential as secondary capacities where if a wind turbine spins faster then the charge controller can convert instead of dumping it out as heat it instead just dumps the energy into the speed of the flywheel so it can be trickled out at a lower voltage with incredible efficiency.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: fascinating mate - thanks for sharing

author: @totherarf
text:
I thought of you this week. Hang on, stay with me .......
It was one of those short videos that get tagged on after you have watched the one you are interested in. It was about something they called "The Google Drive".

Basically a series of 10-1 reduction gears that mean that when you have one rotation of the first gear you have 0.1 rotation on the second .... and there was a BIG stack of them.
What made me think of you was the end comment that if you turned the first gear at 100rpm the last gear would take longer to rotate than the universe has existed ...... but the torque would be enough that if you could anchor it you could move the universe. Alternatively if you go to the other end and managed to crank the slow gear the first gear would be travelling at lightspeed! 

Great conceptualisation I would expect from you!

author: @Nuts-Bolts
text: Hit & Miss engines were hugely important in the days when most of Britain didn’t have mains electricity to drive small equipment, like everything from washing machines to saw benches. Even bicycles shops had them to recharge not only batteries for bikes but also the heavy glass accumulators to power wireless sets.  Very often in towns and cities these engines ran on mains coal-gas.

author: @CCoburn3
text: I remember that in the 70s, they looked into using the electric version in cars. They worked, but people were afraid of what the flywheels would do in a wreck. But these engines would be extremely efficient at storing power in a stationary installation. Replace your battery with a solar panel or wind generator and you can store the generated power for when you need it. Great project.

replies.author: @lostvisitor
replies.text: There is an inherent problem with storing large amounts of power that could spontaneously be released all at once.  It actually takes some effort to cause gasoline to blow up and it won't do it without some help.

replies.author: @CCoburn3
replies.text: @lostvisitor That's what they figured out when they were trying to make a car using flywheels. They decided that the flywheel would have to disintegrate if there was a wreck because if it simply left its mount, the destruction would be immense. But the problem with disintegrating flywheels is that they produce massive amounts of shrapnel.

replies.author: @lostvisitor
replies.text:
@CCoburn3 Lets see.  Hit by 500 kg chunk of steel or 500 kg of sharded metal.

I think we need to do some tests to see what would make a bigger puddle.

replies.author: @CCoburn3
replies.text: @lostvisitor But for stationary installations, flywheels are great for storing energy. It could be cheaper and cleaner than using batteries.

replies.author: @lostvisitor
replies.text:
@CCoburn3 Great idea for short term demands.  Though with magnetic bearings in a vacuum it could hold the spin for a few days.

I wonder if you got enough mass spinning would it create a gravitational effect?
